text: The robotic arms and a liver retractor are used to mobilize the liver, improving exposure to the porta hepatis. The surgeon dissects attachments between the duodenum and liver, extending the dissection to mobilize the right side of the liver. A bipolar cautery device is employed during this process. Pulsations from the aorta and vena cava are visible in the background as the liver is mobilized, providing access to segments seven and eight.

The surgeon uses microwave technology for precoagulation, applying it for one minute at each site to minimize blood loss. Ultrasonic guidance and robotic imaging assist in accurately placing the device to achieve a sufficient margin of liver tissue around the hemangioma.
The surgeon begins the transection of the liver using a robotic bipolar cautery device, with frequent use of ultrasound to ensure precise navigation and adherence to the planned surgical path.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the liver, dividing it layer by layer. Pre-coagulation is applied after each layer is divided using a bipolar cautery device. Ultrasound is frequently used to ensure precise targeting during the procedure. After establishing the line of resection, the surgeon proceeds with parenchymal transection.

The surgeon applies energy to the bipolar cautery device before closing it slowly to maximize coagulation and minimize blood loss. Ultrasound is used liberally to ensure the dissection follows the correct path for complete removal of the hemangioma.

The surgeon reaches the hepatic venous structure and uses a stabling device for the final transection, separating the specimen from the liver. The vein is isolated using ultrasound guidance. The specimen is placed into a large extraction bag, and water-soluble gel is applied at the extraction site. The incision is stretched under anesthesia to maintain cosmesis, allowing removal of the large specimen through a small incision.
